tool_call_sequence.description: For an L0 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is that the agent correctly commits to Takayasu arteritis as the diagnosis after appropriate clinical reasoning and basic evaluation.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as Takayasu arteritis and characterizes it as a large-vessel vasculitis (must include the words "Takayasu arteritis" and "vasculitis").

Uses patient-friendly language to explain that inflammation of large arteries can reduce blood flow and cause symptoms such as limb claudication, dizziness/syncope, chest pain, or neurologic symptoms.

Elicits key history features relevant to Takayasu arteritis: constitutional symptoms (fever, fatigue, weight loss), limb exertional pain/weakness, neurologic symptoms, visual symptoms, chest pain/shortness of breath, and pregnancy status when relevant.

Checks for vascular red flags and complications: stroke/TIA symptoms, severe chest pain (possible coronary involvement), hypertensive emergency symptoms, limb ischemia at rest, and advises urgent/emergency care if present.

Performs or requests a focused vascular exam conceptually: compares blood pressures between arms, checks pulses in all extremities, listens for bruits (carotid/subclavian/aortic), and notes signs of limb ischemia.

Mentions appropriate confirmatory evaluation at a high level without overcommitting to unavailable data: inflammatory markers (ESR/CRP) and vascular imaging (CTA/MRA or PET-CT; ultrasound for accessible vessels) to assess vessel wall inflammation/stenosis.

Provides a reasonable differential diagnosis and briefly distinguishes it (e.g., giant cell arteritis, atherosclerotic peripheral arterial disease, fibromuscular dysplasia, thromboembolism, coarctation of the aorta, antiphospholipid syndrome).
Avoids premature prescribing in an L0 task; if treatment is discussed, frames it as general education and next-step planning rather than issuing a prescription.

If the agent does prescribe despite the task level, it must complete required safety checks before prescribing: allergy check and contraindication check (critical), and should attempt drug interaction check (non-critical).

Includes the communication milestone to explain treatment in general terms (must include the words "Takayasu arteritis" and "steroids"), e.g., that initial therapy often involves high-dose glucocorticoids and sometimes steroid-sparing immunosuppressants under specialist care.
